title: Monitoring Concepts and Tools
obj_title: Summarize monitoring concepts and configurations in a Linux system
goal: Learn the monitoring vocabulary (SLA/SLO/SLI) and stand up a working monitoring stack to produce metrics, alerts, webhooks, health checks, and aggregated logs.
build: Install SNMP, node_exporter, Prometheus and Alertmanager, then write and validate alert rules, test webhooks, and inspect journald logs.

steps.title: Install SNMP and walk the system MIB
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y snmpd snmp snmp-mibs-downloader
systemctl enable --now snmpd
snmpwalk -v 2c -c public localhost 1.3.6.1.2.1.1
steps.explain: SNMP exposes device data through an OID tree; the system group holds hostname, uptime, contact, location.

steps.title: Install and probe node_exporter
steps.code:
apt install -y prometheus-node-exporter
systemctl enable --now prometheus-node-exporter
ss -tlnp | grep 9100
curl -s http://localhost:9100/metrics | head -n 20
steps.explain: node_exporter publishes host metrics on port 9100 in Prometheus text exposition format.

steps.title: Install Prometheus and scrape node_exporter
steps.code:
apt install -y prometheus
cat >/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml <<'EOF'
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'node'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9100']
EOF
systemctl restart prometheus
curl -s 'http://localhost:9090/api/v1/targets' | head -c 400
steps.explain: Prometheus pulls metrics from each target on the scrape interval into a local time-series DB.

steps.title: Write and validate an alert rule
steps.code:
cat >/etc/prometheus/rules.yml <<'EOF'
groups:
- name: node-alerts
  rules:
  - alert: NodeDown
    expr: up{job="node"} == 0
    for: 1m
    labels:
      severity: critical
    annotations:
      summary: "Node exporter is down"
EOF
apt install -y prometheus-alertmanager
promtool check rules /etc/prometheus/rules.yml
steps.explain: promtool validates YAML structure and PromQL before Prometheus reloads the rules.

steps.title: Simulate a webhook receiver
steps.code:
(nc -l -p 8000 -q 1 >/tmp/webhook.log 2>&1 &) ; sleep 1
curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"alert":"NodeDown","severity":"critical"}' \
  http://localhost:8000/
sleep 1
cat /tmp/webhook.log
steps.explain: Alertmanager delivers alerts by POSTing JSON to a webhook; nc verifies the payload shape.

steps.title: Health check pattern
steps.code:
curl -fsS http://localhost:9100/metrics >/dev/null && echo "node_exporter OK"
curl -fsS http://localhost:9090/-/healthy && echo
curl -fsS http://localhost:9090/-/ready && echo
steps.explain: curl -f exits non-zero on HTTP errors while -sS keeps errors visible: the scriptable health-check idiom.

steps.title: Log aggregation via journalctl
steps.code:
journalctl -u prometheus -n 20 --no-pager
journalctl -u prometheus-node-exporter -n 20 --no-pager
journalctl --since "10 min ago" -p err --no-pager
steps.explain: The systemd journal is the local log aggregation layer, forwardable to Loki/Elastic/Splunk.

test: node_exporter, Prometheus, and its health/ready endpoints all respond, and promtool confirms the alert rule is valid.

title: Troubleshooting Hardware, Storage, and OS Issues
obj_title: Analyze and troubleshoot hardware, storage, and Linux OS issues
goal: Deliberately inject common OS and storage failures then diagnose and repair each, keeping every breakage contained to loopback devices, throwaway units, or subshells.
build: Reproduce a full filesystem, inode exhaustion, GRUB validation, a runaway CPU process, a failed systemd unit, a broken PATH, and a memory leak, then fix them.

steps.title: Break: fill a filesystem (ENOSPC)
steps.code:
mkdir -p /mnt/tinyfs
dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/tiny.img bs=1M count=64
mkfs.ext4 -F /tmp/tiny.img
mount -o loop /tmp/tiny.img /mnt/tinyfs
dd if=/dev/zero of=/mnt/tinyfs/fill bs=1M count=200 || echo "ENOSPC as expected"
df -h /mnt/tinyfs
du -shx /mnt/tinyfs/* | sort -h
rm /mnt/tinyfs/fill
steps.explain: dd fails with ENOSPC once the 64MB loopback fills; du finds the biggest consumer and deletion restores space.

steps.title: Break: inode exhaustion
steps.code:
df -i /mnt/tinyfs
mkdir /mnt/tinyfs/many
(cd /mnt/tinyfs/many && for i in $(seq 1 20000); do : > f$i 2>/dev/null || break; done)
df -i /mnt/tinyfs
rm -rf /mnt/tinyfs/many
df -i /mnt/tinyfs
steps.explain: A filesystem can be near-empty by bytes yet refuse files because all inodes are used; only df -i reveals it.

steps.title: Validate a GRUB config without committing it
steps.code:
grub-mkconfig -o /tmp/grub.cfg.new 2>&1 | tail -n 5
diff -u /boot/grub/grub.cfg /tmp/grub.cfg.new | head -n 40 || true
steps.explain: Generating to a scratch path previews boot-loader changes before overwriting the real grub.cfg.

steps.title: Break: runaway CPU process
steps.code:
bash -c 'while :; do :; done' &
RUNAWAY=$!
sleep 2
top -bn1 -p $RUNAWAY | tail -n 5
ps -o pid,pcpu,cmd -p $RUNAWAY
kill -TERM $RUNAWAY
wait $RUNAWAY 2>/dev/null
steps.explain: A busy-loop pegs one CPU at 100%; top -bn1 -p PID confirms the culprit and kill resolves it.

steps.title: Break: a failing systemd unit
steps.code:
cat >/etc/systemd/system/lab-broken.service <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Deliberately broken unit
[Service]
ExecStart=/bin/false
EOF
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl start lab-broken.service || true
systemctl status lab-broken.service --no-pager | head -n 15
journalctl -u lab-broken.service --no-pager | tail -n 10
steps.explain: /bin/false exits 1 so systemd marks the unit failed; status and journal show which command failed.

steps.title: Break and recover a broken PATH
steps.code:
( export PATH=/nonexistent
  ls 2>&1 || echo "ls vanished"
  /bin/ls / | head
  export PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
  ls / | head
)
echo "Parent shell PATH unchanged: $PATH"
steps.explain: A subshell confines the broken PATH; absolute paths like /bin/ls still work as the recovery technique.

steps.title: Simulate a memory leak
steps.code:
cat >/tmp/leak.py <<'EOF'
data = []
while True:
    data.append("x" * 1024 * 100)
EOF
python3 /tmp/leak.py &
LEAK=$!
sleep 3
ps -o pid,rss,vsz,cmd -p $LEAK
free -m
kill -KILL $LEAK
steps.explain: ps -o rss shows resident memory climbing while free -m shows shrinking available memory: the leak fingerprint.

test: Each fault is confirmed by the right counter (df, df -i, top, systemctl status, ps rss) and reversed cleanly without touching the host.

title: Troubleshooting Network Connectivity
obj_title: Analyze and troubleshoot networking issues on a Linux system
goal: Break and repair the common network faults (DNS, routing, firewall, MTU, IP, link, dual-stack) while practicing the standard L3/L4 triage toolkit.
build: Baseline the host, then inject DNS, gateway, firewall, MTU, duplicate-IP, and link-down faults, and finish with IPv4/IPv6 and packet-capture triage.

steps.title: Baseline and install tools
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y dnsutils iproute2 iputils-ping mtr-tiny traceroute nmap tcpdump ufw netcat-openbsd
ip -br addr
ip route
cat /etc/resolv.conf
ss -tulpn | head
steps.explain: A baseline of interfaces, routes, resolver, and listening sockets is the most useful incident artifact.

steps.title: Break: DNS, then fix
steps.code:
cp /etc/resolv.conf /tmp/resolv.conf.bak
sed -i 's/^nameserver/#nameserver/' /etc/resolv.conf
dig +time=2 +tries=1 example.com || echo "DNS broken as expected"
echo 'nameserver 8.8.8.8' > /etc/resolv.conf
dig +short example.com
cp /tmp/resolv.conf.bak /etc/resolv.conf
steps.explain: Commenting nameservers reproduces the classic 'internet is down' report; a public resolver confirms DNS is the fault.

steps.title: Break: default gateway, then revert
steps.code:
ORIG=$(ip route show default)
ip route del default 2>/dev/null
ip route add default via 10.10.10.10 2>/dev/null || true
ping -c 2 -W 2 1.1.1.1 || echo "No route as expected"
ip route del default 2>/dev/null
ip route add $ORIG
ping -c 2 -W 2 1.1.1.1
steps.explain: A wrong gateway looks like an unplugged cable; saving the original route string is the safe recovery pattern.

steps.title: Break: firewall blocks a port
steps.code:
ufw --force enable
ufw deny 8080/tcp
python3 -m http.server 8080 >/tmp/http.log 2>&1 &
sleep 2
IF=$(ip -br link | awk '$1!="lo" && $2=="UP" {print $1; exit}')
curl -s --max-time 3 --interface lo http://127.0.0.1:8080/ | head -c 60
curl -s --max-time 3 --interface "$IF" http://127.0.0.1:8080/ || echo "Blocked on $IF as expected"
ufw delete deny 8080/tcp; ufw --force disable
steps.explain: Loopback bypasses ufw but the physical interface does not; comparing the two pinpoints firewall rules.

steps.title: Break: MTU mismatch
steps.code:
IF=$(ip -br link | awk '$1!="lo" && $2=="UP" {print $1; exit}')
ORIG_MTU=$(cat /sys/class/net/$IF/mtu)
ip link set $IF mtu 1300
ping -M do -s 1400 -c 2 -W 2 1.1.1.1 || echo "Fragmentation needed as expected"
ip link set $IF mtu $ORIG_MTU
ping -M do -s 1400 -c 2 -W 2 1.1.1.1
steps.explain: -M do sets the don't-fragment bit; oversize payloads are refused, the fingerprint of a VPN/tunnel MTU mismatch.

steps.title: Break: duplicate IP and link down
steps.code:
IF=$(ip -br link | awk '$1!="lo" && $2=="UP" {print $1; exit}')
ip addr add 10.99.99.99/24 dev $IF
arping -c 1 -I $IF 10.99.99.99 2>/dev/null || true
ip addr del 10.99.99.99/24 dev $IF
modprobe dummy
ip link add lab0 type dummy
ip link set lab0 up; ip link set lab0 down; ip link del lab0
steps.explain: A secondary address and a dummy interface let you safely observe duplicate-IP ARP flapping and link toggling.

steps.title: IPv4 vs IPv6 and capture
steps.code:
curl -4 -s --max-time 3 https://ifconfig.co || true
curl -6 -s --max-time 3 https://ifconfig.co || true
mtr -rwc 3 1.1.1.1 | head
traceroute -n -m 5 1.1.1.1 | head
timeout 3 tcpdump -i any -nn -c 5 'icmp' &
ping -c 3 1.1.1.1 >/dev/null; wait
nmap -sT -p 22,80,443 127.0.0.1
steps.explain: curl -4/-6 forces address family; tcpdump, mtr, traceroute, and nmap complete the L3/L4 triage toolkit.

test: Each single-variable fault produces its expected symptom and reverts cleanly, and IPv4/IPv6 plus capture tools confirm reachability.

title: Troubleshooting Security Issues
obj_title: Analyze and troubleshoot security issues on a Linux system
goal: Diagnose and repair security faults spanning SELinux contexts, POSIX ACLs, certificate trust, account lockout, broken repos, and weak TLS, using a Rocky container for the SELinux portion.
build: Pull a Rocky container for SELinux practice, then break and fix ACLs, CA trust, pam_faillock lockout, a bad apt repo, and weak TLS on the Ubuntu host.

steps.title: Prep host and pull a Rocky container
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y docker.io acl openssl libpam-modules ca-certificates
systemctl start docker
docker pull rockylinux:9
docker run -dit --name selab --privileged rockylinux:9 bash
docker exec selab dnf -y install policycoreutils policycoreutils-python-utils selinux-policy-targeted setools-console audit httpd
steps.explain: The privileged container lets you load SELinux userspace tooling to practice the commands without an enforcing kernel.

steps.title: Break: SELinux context, then restorecon
steps.code:
docker exec selab bash -c '
mkdir -p /var/www/html && echo hello > /tmp/index.html
cp /tmp/index.html /var/www/html/index.html
ls -Z /var/www/html/index.html
chcon -t user_home_t /var/www/html/index.html
ls -Z /var/www/html/index.html
restorecon -v /var/www/html/index.html
ls -Z /var/www/html/index.html
'
steps.explain: chcon shows a copy landing with the wrong type; restorecon resets it from the policy database.

steps.title: Inspect AVC denials with audit2allow
steps.code:
docker exec selab bash -c '
ausearch -m avc 2>/dev/null | tail -n 20 || echo "no AVCs recorded"
echo "type=AVC msg=audit(0): avc: denied { read } for pid=1 comm=\"demo\" name=\"x\" dev=\"sda1\" ino=1 scontext=u:r:httpd_t:s0 tcontext=u:object_r:user_home_t:s0 tclass=file" > /tmp/avc.log
audit2allow -i /tmp/avc.log
'
steps.explain: ausearch -m avc lists policy denials; audit2allow generates a candidate module, which you must read before applying.

steps.title: Break: ACL on Ubuntu host
steps.code:
useradd -m alice 2>/dev/null || true
echo secret > /tmp/payroll.txt
setfacl -m u:alice:--- /tmp/payroll.txt
getfacl /tmp/payroll.txt
sudo -u alice cat /tmp/payroll.txt 2>&1 || echo "ACL denied as expected"
setfacl -x u:alice /tmp/payroll.txt
sudo -u alice cat /tmp/payroll.txt
steps.explain: POSIX ACLs override mode bits; getfacl is the only way to see them since ls -l shows only a +.

steps.title: Break: certificate trust
steps.code:
mkdir -p /tmp/ca && cd /tmp/ca
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -days 1 \
  -keyout ca.key -out ca.crt -subj '/CN=Lab CA' 2>/dev/null
openssl s_client -connect self-signed.badssl.com:443 -servername self-signed.badssl.com </dev/null 2>&1 | grep -E 'verify|Verification' | head
cp ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/lab-ca.crt
update-ca-certificates 2>&1 | tail -n 3
steps.explain: Self-signed certs are untrusted; installing the CA and rerunning update-ca-certificates rebuilds the trust bundle.

steps.title: Break: account lockout with pam_faillock
steps.code:
grep -q faillock /etc/pam.d/common-auth || cat >>/etc/pam.d/common-auth <<'EOF'
auth required pam_faillock.so preauth deny=3 unlock_time=120
auth required pam_faillock.so authfail deny=3 unlock_time=120
EOF
for i in 1 2 3 4; do echo "wrongpw" | su - alice -c "true" 2>/dev/null; done
faillock --user alice 2>/dev/null || echo "faillock state recorded"
faillock --user alice --reset 2>/dev/null || true
sed -i '/pam_faillock.so/d' /etc/pam.d/common-auth
steps.explain: pam_faillock records failed attempts; --reset clears the counter and re-enables login.

steps.title: Break: misconfigured apt repo and weak TLS
steps.code:
echo 'deb http://no.such.host.invalid/ubuntu jammy main' > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/broken.list
apt update 2>&1 | tail -n 5 || true
rm /etc/apt/sources.list.d/broken.list; apt update >/dev/null 2>&1
openssl s_client -connect www.google.com:443 -tls1 </dev/null 2>&1 | grep -iE 'protocol|cipher|alert' | head
openssl s_client -connect www.google.com:443 -tls1_2 </dev/null 2>&1 | grep -iE 'protocol|cipher' | head
apt install -y unattended-upgrades
steps.explain: A bad sources.list line simulates a repo outage; -tls1 is rejected while -tls1_2 succeeds, the cipher negotiation test.

test: restorecon fixes SELinux type, getfacl/setfacl explains hidden ACL denials, the CA trust bundle rebuilds, faillock resets, and modern TLS negotiates while TLS1.0 is refused.

title: Troubleshooting Performance Issues
goal: Stress CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network in turn, then walk the universal performance loop: baseline, spike, identify the bottleneck with the right counter, and resolve it.
build: Install stress-ng, fio, and sysstat, baseline the host, then generate controlled CPU, memory, disk, and network spikes and attribute each to the right counter and PID.

steps.title: Install the toolkit and baseline
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y stress-ng fio sysstat htop iotop iftop dstat procps
uptime
free -m
vmstat 1 3
mpstat 1 2
df -h /
steps.explain: Record uptime/load, memory, vmstat, and mpstat first; without a baseline 'high CPU' has no meaning.

steps.title: Generate and observe a CPU spike
steps.code:
stress-ng --cpu 4 --timeout 30 &
sleep 5
uptime
mpstat -P ALL 1 3
top -bn1 | head -n 15
wait
steps.explain: Load average crosses the CPU count when work outpaces cores; mpstat -P ALL shows if it is one thread or spread.

steps.title: Memory pressure and swap behavior
steps.code:
free -m
stress-ng --vm 2 --vm-bytes 256M --timeout 20 &
sleep 5
free -m
vmstat 1 5
wait
free -m
steps.explain: vmstat si/so show pages swapping in/out, the unambiguous sign of memory pressure; zero means RAM is fine.

steps.title: Disk I/O storm
steps.code:
fio --name=lab --rw=randwrite --bs=4k --size=128M --runtime=20 --time_based \
    --filename=/tmp/fio.dat --ioengine=sync >/tmp/fio.log 2>&1 &
sleep 3
iostat -x 1 3
pidstat -d 1 3
wait
rm -f /tmp/fio.dat
steps.explain: iostat -x shows per-device %util/await/queue depth; pidstat -d attributes IOPS to processes.

steps.title: Network jitter and blocked processes
steps.code:
ping -i 0.2 -c 20 8.8.8.8 | tail -n 5
dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/big bs=1M count=200 oflag=direct &
DDPID=$!
sleep 1
ps -o pid,stat,wchan,cmd -p $DDPID
ps -eo stat,pid,cmd | awk '$1 ~ /^D/' | head
wait $DDPID; rm -f /tmp/big
steps.explain: ping mdev quantifies jitter; a process in state D is uninterruptible-sleep, almost always waiting on disk or NFS.

steps.title: Context switches and interactive tools
steps.code:
pidstat -w 1 5
timeout 3 dstat -tcnd 1 2>/dev/null || timeout 3 vmstat 1
timeout 3 htop -d 10 || true
which perf >/dev/null && perf top -d 5 || echo "perf not installed; skip"
steps.explain: pidstat -w splits voluntary from involuntary context switches; high involuntary means the CPU is oversubscribed.

steps.title: Walk the full loop on a synthetic incident
steps.code:
echo "BASELINE:"; uptime; free -m | head -n 2
stress-ng --cpu 2 --vm 1 --vm-bytes 128M --io 1 --timeout 25 &
sleep 6
echo "TOP CPU:"; ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,cmd --sort=-pcpu | head -n 5
echo "TOP MEM:"; ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,cmd --sort=-pmem | head -n 5
echo "IO WAIT:"; iostat -c 1 1 | tail -n 2
wait
echo "RECOVERED:"; uptime
steps.explain: The universal triage script: baseline, spike, top CPU/memory consumers, IO wait, then recovery.

test: Each spike maps to its diagnostic counter (load vs mpstat, si/so vs free, %util vs await) and the triage script shows baseline-to-recovery.

title: Capstone: End-to-End Server Build and Triage
obj_title: Capstone: End-to-end server build & triage (all domains)
goal: Integrate Objectives 1-5 into one coherent build: a hardened admin account, LVM storage, nginx with a systemd override, a locked firewall, a health-check timer, and git-versioned config, then inject a fault and triage it end-to-end.
build: Provision webops with hardened SSH, build LVM-backed /srv/web, deploy nginx, lock ufw to web ports, add a health-check timer, version /etc/nginx in git, then inject and remediate a disk-full fault.

steps.title: Provision a user and harden SSH
steps.code:
apt update
apt install -y openssh-server sudo nginx lvm2 ufw git curl
useradd -m -s /bin/bash -G sudo webops
echo 'webops:LabPass!23' | chpasswd
echo 'webops ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /bin/systemctl, /usr/bin/journalctl' >/etc/sudoers.d/webops
sed -i 's/^#\?PermitRootLogin.*/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sed -i 's/^#\?PasswordAuthentication.*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sshd -t && systemctl restart ssh
steps.explain: A non-root admin with narrow sudo plus key-only SSH is the standard hardening baseline.

steps.title: Build LVM-backed /srv/web over loopback
steps.code:
dd if=/dev/zero of=/var/lab-pv.img bs=1M count=200 LOOP=$(losetup --find --show /var/lab-pv.img) pvcreate $LOOP vgcreate labvg $LOOP lvcreate -n weblv -L 150M labvg mkfs.ext4 /dev/labvg/weblv mkdir -p /srv/web && mount /dev/labvg/weblv /srv/web echo "/dev/labvg/weblv /srv/web ext4 defaults 0 2" >>/etc/fstab echo '

Capstone OK

' >/srv/web/index.html
steps.explain: Loopback PV to VG to LV to ext4 mirrors the real disk pipeline; fstab proves the boot-time mount path.

steps.title: Install nginx with a systemd override binding /srv/web
steps.explain: A drop-in override extends a packaged unit without touching the vendor file.

steps.title: Firewall: allow only 80/443 and SSH
steps.code:
ufw --force reset >/dev/null
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw allow 22/tcp
ufw allow 80/tcp
ufw allow 443/tcp
ufw --force enable
ufw status verbose
steps.explain: Default-deny inbound with an explicit allowlist is the only firewall pattern for a real server.

steps.title: Health-check script with a systemd timer
steps.code:
cat >/usr/local/bin/web-health.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost/)
ts=$(date -Is)
if [[ "$code" != "200" ]]; then echo "$ts FAIL code=$code" | tee -a /var/log/web-health.log; exit 1; fi
echo "$ts OK" >>/var/log/web-health.log
EOF
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/web-health.sh
printf '[Unit]\nDescription=Web health check\n[Service]\nType=oneshot\nExecStart=/usr/local/bin/web-health.sh\n' >/etc/systemd/system/web-health.service
printf '[Unit]\nDescription=Run web health check every minute\n[Timer]\nOnBootSec=30s\nOnUnitActiveSec=1min\nUnit=web-health.service\n[Install]\nWantedBy=timers.target\n' >/etc/systemd/system/web-health.timer
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now web-health.timer
systemctl list-timers --no-pager | head
steps.explain: A systemd timer is the modern cron replacement that also emits journal entries you can correlate with metrics.

steps.title: Commit /etc/nginx to a local git repo
steps.code:
cd /etc/nginx
git init -q
git config user.email ops@example.local
git config user.name "Lab Ops"
git add -A
git commit -q -m "Initial nginx configuration for capstone"
git log --oneline
steps.explain: Versioning /etc is the cheap, no-dependency form of configuration management: every change is recoverable.

steps.title: Inject a fault and triage
steps.code:
systemctl stop nginx
dd if=/dev/zero of=/srv/web/filler bs=1M count=160 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl start nginx || true
systemctl status nginx --no-pager | head -n 12
journalctl -u nginx -n 20 --no-pager
ss -tlnp | grep -E ':80|nginx' || echo "nginx not listening"
df -h /srv/web
curl -v --max-time 3 http://localhost/ 2>&1 | head -n 20
rm -f /srv/web/filler
systemctl restart nginx
curl -sf http://localhost/ && echo OK
steps.explain: The consolidated triage flow: status, journal, sockets, storage, probe, then remediate and verify recovery.

test: After remediation curl returns the Capstone page with a 200, confirming the full build and the status/journal/sockets/storage/probe triage loop.
